source_note: Rank definitions are paraphrased from the official IELTS Writing Task 2 Band Descriptors (Public Version, updated May 2023, British Council / IDP / Cambridge). Each skill below maps to one of the 4 official criteria: Task Response, Coherence & Cohesion, Lexical Resource, Grammatical Range & Accuracy. Ranks are independent of IELTS bands 1-9 — they are a 5-step coaching ladder (1=beginner, 5=advanced) used to track this learner's mastery of one specific sub-skill over time, not a test score.

skills.description: Addressing every part of the prompt, not just the easiest part to write about.

ranks.1: Tackles the prompt only minimally or tangentially; large parts of the question are not addressed at all.
ranks.2: Addresses the main parts of the prompt but treats some parts far more fully than others; at least one part is noticeably underdeveloped.
ranks.3: Addresses the main parts of the prompt, though coverage is uneven — some required elements are present but thin.
ranks.4: Addresses all parts of the prompt appropriately and sufficiently, with only occasional omissions or lapses.
ranks.5: Addresses all parts of the prompt fully and explores them in depth; every element the prompt asks for is clearly and proportionately covered.

skills.description: Presenting one clear, identifiable stance and maintaining it throughout the essay.

ranks.1: No relevant position can be identified, or the reader cannot tell what the writer actually believes.
ranks.2: A position is discernible but the reader has to search for it; it may be inconsistent or contradicted elsewhere in the essay.
ranks.3: A position is presented and is directly relevant to the prompt, though it may be unclear, unjustified, or stated only once without reinforcement.
ranks.4: A clear and well-developed position is presented and maintained, answering the question directly.
ranks.5: A clear and fully developed position is presented which directly and confidently answers the question throughout the entire response.

skills.description: Extending ideas with explanation, reasoning, and support rather than just stating them.

ranks.1: Ideas, where identifiable, lack any development or support; points are simply listed.
ranks.2: Main ideas are difficult to identify and what is identifiable may be repetitive or unsupported.
ranks.3: Main ideas are present but are limited and not sufficiently developed; some supporting detail is missing or only loosely relevant.
ranks.4: Ideas are relevant, extended and supported, though there may be a tendency to over-generalise or lack precision in places.
ranks.5: Ideas are relevant, fully extended and well supported throughout; any lapses in development are extremely rare.

skills.description: A conclusion that genuinely ties back to and reflects every dimension raised in the prompt, not just a generic restatement.

ranks.1: No real conclusion is present, or it is entirely disconnected from the body of the essay.
ranks.2: A conclusion is present but restates only the easiest or most general part of the argument, ignoring other dimensions of the prompt.
ranks.3: The conclusion summarises the writer's overall position but omits explicit reference to one or more specific elements the prompt asked about.
ranks.4: The conclusion effectively synthesises the main ideas and reflects most dimensions of the prompt, with only minor omissions.
ranks.5: The conclusion fully synthesises the response, explicitly reflecting every dimension of the prompt and reinforcing the position with no gaps in coverage.

skills.description: Ideas building on each other in a clear order so the response can be followed without effort.

ranks.1: There is no apparent logical organisation; ideas are discernible but difficult to relate to one another.
ranks.2: Information and ideas are evident but not arranged coherently; there is no clear progression within the response.
ranks.3: Organisation is evident but is not wholly logical; there is some underlying coherence but progression is inconsistent.
ranks.4: Information and ideas are logically organised with a clear progression throughout; a few lapses may occur but are minor.
ranks.5: The message can be followed effortlessly; ideas progress with skilfully managed logical sequencing throughout.

skills.description: Using paragraphs to organise distinct ideas, with one central topic per paragraph.

ranks.1: There is no paragraphing, or paragraphing has no clear main topic.
ranks.2: Paragraphing may be missing or inadequate; where present, the central topic is often unclear.
ranks.3: Paragraphing is attempted but may not always be logical, or the central topic of a paragraph is not always clear.
ranks.4: Paragraphing is used sufficiently and appropriately to support overall coherence.
ranks.5: Paragraphing is skilfully managed, with each paragraph clearly and purposefully organised around one central idea.

skills.description: Using linking words, reference, and substitution accurately to connect ideas without overusing mechanical connectors.

ranks.1: There is minimal or no use of cohesive devices; relationships between ideas are unclear or unmarked.
ranks.2: Basic cohesive devices are used but may be inaccurate, repetitive, or fail to indicate a logical relationship between ideas.
ranks.3: Cohesive devices are used to some effect, but cohesion within or between sentences may be faulty or mechanical due to misuse or overuse.
ranks.4: A range of cohesive devices including reference and substitution is used flexibly, though some inaccuracies or over/under-use may occur.
ranks.5: Cohesion is managed so well that it rarely attracts attention; reference and substitution are used naturally and precisely throughout.

skills.description: Using a sufficiently wide variety of vocabulary appropriate to the topic, rather than relying on the same words repeatedly.

ranks.1: Vocabulary is extremely limited, essentially relying on memorised phrases or a few recognisable words.
ranks.2: Vocabulary is basic and used repetitively; the resource is limited and inadequate for the task.
ranks.3: Vocabulary is minimally adequate for the task but the range does not permit much variation in expression.
ranks.4: A sufficient range of vocabulary is used to allow some flexibility and precision, with some ability to use less common items.
ranks.5: A wide range of vocabulary is used accurately and appropriately with natural and sophisticated control.

skills.description: Selecting the most accurate word for the intended meaning rather than an approximate or vague alternative.

ranks.1: Control of word choice is very limited and errors predominate, severely impeding meaning.
ranks.2: Inappropriate word choice may impede meaning; lexical chunks may be used inaccurately or inappropriately.
ranks.3: Meaning is generally clear despite a restricted range or lack of precision in word choice; frequent simplification is apparent.
ranks.4: Word choice is generally precise with only occasional inaccuracies in collocation or appropriacy.
ranks.5: Word choice is precise and natural throughout, with skilful use of uncommon or idiomatic items where appropriate.

skills.description: Spelling words correctly and forming words (e.g. noun/verb/adjective forms) accurately.

ranks.1: There is no apparent control of word formation or spelling.
ranks.2: Errors in spelling and word formation are noticeable and may impede meaning.
ranks.3: There are some errors in spelling or word formation, but these do not impede communication.
ranks.4: Occasional errors in spelling or word formation may occur but have minimal impact on communication.
ranks.5: Minor errors in spelling and word formation are extremely rare and have no real impact on communication.

skills.description: Using a mix of simple and complex sentence structures rather than relying on one repeated pattern.

ranks.1: There is little or no evidence of varied sentence forms; simple sentences predominate almost entirely.
ranks.2: A very limited range of structures is used; subordinate clauses are rare and simple sentences predominate.
ranks.3: A mix of simple and complex sentence forms is used but flexibility is limited; complex sentences are attempted but tend to be faulty.
ranks.4: A variety of complex structures is used with some flexibility and accuracy.
ranks.5: A wide range of structures is used with full flexibility and control, appropriate to the meaning intended.

skills.description: Producing grammatically correct sentences consistently, including subject-verb agreement, tense, and articles.

ranks.1: Errors in grammar predominate and prevent most meaning from coming through.
ranks.2: Grammatical errors are frequent and may impede meaning.
ranks.3: Errors in grammar occur and may be frequent, sometimes causing the reader some difficulty.
ranks.4: Grammar is generally well controlled; error-free sentences are frequent, though a few errors may persist.
ranks.5: A wide range of structures is used with full accuracy; minor errors occur only as rare slips with no impact on communication.

skills.description: Using punctuation (commas, full stops, apostrophes) correctly to support clear sentence boundaries and meaning.

ranks.1: Punctuation is largely absent or used in a way that prevents meaning from being understood.
ranks.2: Punctuation is often faulty or inadequate.
ranks.3: Punctuation may be faulty and occasionally affects clarity.
ranks.4: Punctuation is generally well managed, with only occasional, non-systematic errors.
ranks.5: Punctuation is used appropriately and accurately throughout, attracting no negative attention.
